metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a count-and-content assertion to the AC5 proof that independently verifies BOTH gold_set entries exist with the correct labels, e.g.: `python -c "import yaml; gs=yaml.safe_load(open('src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml'))['gold_set']; assert sum(1 for e in gs if e.get('label')=='useful' and 'operator-attested' in str(e)) >= 1; assert sum(1 for e in gs if e.get('label')=='not_useful') >= 1"`
findings.finding: AC5's proving command does not independently verify that TWO gold_set entries with the carve-out content actually exist. The proof given is (a) `grep -qi 'operator-attested' ...eval.yaml` (only proves the term appears somewhere) and (b) the AC4 `validate_eval_spec` call (validates schema structure, not gold_set semantic content). An executor could add one entry (or zero entries) with any label and both commands would still pass — satisfying the letter of the proof without demonstrating the two-ways carve-out claim.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC5 proof block: `python -c "import yaml; gs=yaml.safe_load(open('src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml'))['gold_set']; labels=[e['label'] for e in gs]; assert labels.count('useful')>=1 and labels.count('not_useful')>=1, 'gold_set missing required carve-out entries'"`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC that verifies the `docs/` section (e.g., a `grep` for a link from `completion_verifier.md` to a named docs path) or fold its content into the ADR and remove the separate docs/ section from scope.
findings.finding: The Scope IN names 'a short `docs/` section linked from `completion_verifier.md`' as a deliverable, but no AC verifies this section's existence or content — it can be silently omitted without any proof command failing. This is an unenumerated out-of-AC deliverable.
findings.location: Scope (IN) — 'a short docs/ section linked from completion_verifier.md'

findings.finding: The plan's four new eval dataset cases for `completion-verifier.eval.yaml` add `[operator-attested]`-tagged AC items. The existing `test_new_eval_specs.py` runs `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` against the `completion-verifier` spec — this test will re-run and cover the new cases automatically. However, `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` enforces that the dataset is 'balanced' between `FIRE_EXPECTS` and `NOFIRE_EXPECTS` labels. The current spec has 3 `fail` + 3 `pass` cases. Adding 4 new cases (a: pass, b: fail, c: pass, d: fail = 2 pass + 2 fail) keeps balance. BUT the plan also calls for updating the `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` rubric (AC5) to be case-conditional. This rubric change is a textual change to the eval YAML, not a new scorer — `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` does NOT validate rubric text. The plan's proof for AC5 is only `grep -qi 'operator-attested' …eval.yaml`, which does not confirm the case-conditional logic actually works correctly for the new cases.

The AC4c eval case (CV-attestation-with-injection) requires 'a scorer/rubric asserting the PASS was reached by crediting the factual specific while ignoring the command,' but the plan never specifies which scorer name, whether it is a new deterministic scorer or an LLM-judge, or how it is wired. No matching scorer name exists in eval_scorers.py REGISTRY. The `expect: pass` + `no_false_fail_on_complete` scorer pair would fire only on PASS/FAIL verdict — it cannot distinguish *why* a PASS was reached (factual credit vs. command obeying).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the 'short `docs/` section' in Scope is the ADR itself or a separate file, and if separate, add an AC proof (e.g., `test -f docs/<path>.md`) so it cannot be silently omitted.
findings.finding: The Scope IN-list names 'a short `docs/` section linked from `completion_verifier.md`' as a deliverable, but no AC verifies this section exists or has substantive content — it can be silently dropped without failing any grep/test proof.
findings.location: Scope IN bullet: 'a short `docs/` section linked from `completion_verifier.md`'; also AC7
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is that the ADR IS the `docs/` section, remove the separate mention from Scope to eliminate ambiguity. If it is genuinely a separate file, add to AC7's proof chain: `test -f docs/<chosen-path>.md && grep -qi 'operator-attested' docs/<chosen-path>.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exact mechanism for documenting per-finding `remediation` in `completion_verdict.schema.json` without touching `common.schema.json` (e.g., wrap the `$ref` with a sibling `description`, or use an `allOf` overlay), and add a schema-file grep to the AC3 proof (e.g., `grep -q remediation src/rebar/schemas/completion_verdict.schema.json`) so a stub change at the array level cannot pass.
findings.finding: AC3 (Scope section) asserts the plan will 'document the per-finding remediation in the findings items description' of completion_verdict.schema.json WITHOUT touching common.schema.json — but the schema's findings.items is currently a bare {"$ref": "common.schema.json#/$defs/finding"} with no inline description slot. JSON Schema 2020-12 does allow sibling keywords alongside $ref, but the plan does not specify the mechanism (wrap the $ref, add a sibling description, or just update the array-level description). The AC3 proof grep only targets contracts.py, not the schema file, so a stub change (e.g., adding 'remediation' only to the array-level description) would pass the proof while the per-finding field is not actually documented in the items shape.
findings.location: Scope section ('completion_verdict.schema.json — document the per-finding remediation in the findings items description') and AC3 proof command
findings.suggested_fix: In completion_verdict.schema.json, change `"items": { "$ref": "common.schema.json#/$defs/finding" }` to `"items": { "$ref": "common.schema.json#/$defs/finding", "description": "… remediation: optional str …" }` (JSON Schema 2020-12 allows sibling keywords). Then add `grep -q remediation src/rebar/schemas/completion_verdict.schema.json` to the AC3 proof command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the AC6 CLI invocation from `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` to `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` (matching the actual `rebar prompt eval <id>` subcommand documented throughout the codebase).
findings.finding: AC6 names the CLI invocation `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier`, but the actual command is `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` — `rebar-eval` does not exist as a binary or subcommand anywhere in the codebase.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` with `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` in AC6. The live run invocation then becomes: `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` (needs `[eval]` extra + credentials).

findings.finding: The AC6 proof instruction names `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` as the CLI command to run the live eval, but no `rebar-eval` binary exists — `pyproject.toml` registers only `rebar` and `rebar-mcp`; the correct command is `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier`.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` with `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` in AC6; the `--live` flag (if required for a live run) should also be confirmed against `rebar prompt eval --help`.

findings.finding: AC3's text requires three things — the `remediation` field in `VerdictFinding`, the schema documentation, AND the prompt instructing the verifier to populate it for an unmet operator-attested criterion — but the Proof only covers the first two (unit test + grep contracts.py + make test). No grep or check verifies that `completion_verifier.md` actually contains the remediation-population instruction, leaving that sub-requirement unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3's Proof line: `&& grep -qi 'remediation' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md` so the prompt requirement is mechanically verified alongside the code requirement.

findings.finding: AC5's verification method is 'Verified by reading the eval spec; covered by the AC5 validate_eval_spec command' — but the specific claim (that the grounding rubric accepts ticket-text citation for operator-attested findings) is a semantic/behavioral property that validate_eval_spec does not test, leaving AC5 with no machine-executable proving command.
findings.location: AC5 (grounding rubric relaxation) — 'Verified by reading the eval spec'

findings.finding: The plan instructs the verifier prompt to populate `remediation` on `VerdictFinding` for an unmet operator-attested criterion (AC3: 'the prompt instructs the verifier to populate it'), but the existing `completion_verifier.md` has no `remediation` field in its output schema description, and the new prompt additions are only described in the plan's prose — the structural link between the prompt's output schema section and the new `remediation` field is never specified (e.g., whether `remediation` is added to the 'Each finding:' bullet list in the prompt).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `## Verdict and findings` section under 'Each finding:': '- `remediation` (operator-attested unmet only): the accepted next move — record the reference (change URL/id), the observed outcome (votes/logs/console value), and when, as a comment on the ticket; absent for codebase-verifiable findings and for MET criteria.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC5 HOW the rubric conditionality is implemented (e.g., a per-case `rubric_override:` field, a `case_type:` tag the judge is instructed to branch on, or inline conditional instructions in the rubric text), and strengthen the proof to verify the two gold_set entries are present by content (not just that 'operator-attested' appears in the file).
findings.finding: AC5 asserts the `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` llm-judge rubric will be 'made case-conditional' (different grounding bars for operator-attested vs codebase-verifiable cases), but the eval.yaml scorer structure supports only a single `rubric:` string field. The AC5 proof (`grep -qi 'operator-attested' ...eval.yaml`) only verifies the text 'operator-attested' appears somewhere in the file; `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` validates schema structure, not semantic conditionality. A rubric string that mentions 'operator-attested' once (even in a note) would pass both proof commands while not actually implementing case-conditional scoring.
findings.location: AC5 — `completion-verifier.eval.yaml` llm-judge rubric conditionality claim and proof command

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fifth eval case `CV-malformed-tag-fallback` (expect: fail) — an item tagged `[operator_attested]` (underscore) or another named near-miss with only a ticket-comment attestation and no matching code, verifying the verifier treats it as codebase-verifiable and finds it NOT MET.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly specifies that a malformed/near-miss tag (e.g., `[operator_attested]` with an underscore, or any unrecognized bracketed token) MUST fall back to codebase-verifiable — but AC4 provides no eval case exercising this boundary. AC4(d) only tests an untagged item; there is no case where a plausible-but-wrong tag variant is present and the verifier must correctly reject the promotion to operator-attested. An LLM verifier could plausibly accept `[operator_attested]` as equivalent to `[operator-attested]` and apply the more permissive bar, silently giving a wrong verdict — and the eval suite would not catch it.
findings.location: AC4 (eval cases) and 'AC-authoring convention' section (malformed/near-miss→codebase-verifiable rule)
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to `completion-verifier.eval.yaml`:
```yaml
- id: CV-malformed-tag-fallback
  description: Near-miss tag (underscore variant) must NOT be promoted to operator-attested
  ticket_context: |
    - [ ] [operator_attested] Deploy completed and verified live.
    Comment: Deployed at 14:32 UTC, run id abc123.
  repo_snapshot: {}  # no code evidence
  expect: fail
```
This exercises the named near-miss example from the spec and proves the safe-default rule holds under the live eval (AC6).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add a display-path update (in `transition_close.py` and/or `_llm_commands.py`) to the Scope IN list that reads per-finding `remediation` and surfaces it alongside each failing criterion, or (b) drop the per-finding `remediation` field from `VerdictFinding` and instead strengthen the per-finding `detail` field's prompt instruction to include the operator-attested remediation specifics — the `detail` field is already displayed per-finding.
findings.finding: The plan adds a per-finding `remediation: str | None` to `VerdictFinding` (AC3) that is populated by the LLM for unmet operator-attested criteria, but no existing or planned consumer reads this field from individual findings. Both current display paths (`transition_close.py:208` and `_llm_commands.py:577`) read only the top-level `result.get('remediation')`, and the Scope IN list does not include updating these display paths. The field would be generated and round-trip-tested but never surfaced to users — a YAGNI gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the `transition_close.py` finding-display loop (lines 200–214) to also print `f.get('remediation')` when present, so the per-finding operator-attested guidance is surfaced beneath each failing criterion. Alternatively, fold the per-criterion guidance into the finding's `detail` field (no new field needed) and update only the prompt.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rename the new per-finding field (e.g., `remediation_hint` or `next_step`) OR document in AC3/the schema description why both `remediation` fields coexist and how consumers should distinguish them.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two distinct fields both named `remediation` at different levels of the completion verdict data model — one per-finding on `VerdictFinding` (new, AC3) and one top-level on the verdict set by `reconcile_verdict` (existing, kept) — with no disambiguation guidance, creating a naming collision risk for downstream consumers and future maintainers.
findings.location: Scope IN: `src/rebar/llm/contracts.py`; AC3; plan sentence 'The top-level `remediation` set by `reconcile_verdict` is kept; this is additive.'
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the new VerdictFinding field to `remediation_hint: str | None` and update the schema description and prompt instruction accordingly, keeping the top-level `remediation` name unchanged — this eliminates the collision with a one-word change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md` is a NEW FILE creation (not an edit to an existing file), and verify there is no numbering conflict (0042 is also absent — check whether the next available ADR number is 0043 or higher).
findings.finding: ADR `docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md` does not exist in the repository. The ADR directory currently ends at `0041-*` entries. AC7's proof shell script (`test -f "$A"`) would immediately fail, and the Scope IN list names this as a deliverable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Scope section that ADR 0043 is a new file creation. Verify the correct next available ADR number by checking the full directory listing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan how `completion_verdict.schema.json`'s `findings` items definition will be updated to include the per-finding `remediation` field without modifying `common.schema.json` — e.g., whether to use `allOf: [{$ref:...}, {properties:{remediation:{...}}}]` or sibling-keyword annotation.
findings.finding: The plan says to 'document the per-finding `remediation` in the `findings` items description' of `completion_verdict.schema.json`, but the `findings` array items are currently defined as a bare `$ref` (`{ "$ref": "common.schema.json#/$defs/finding" }`) with no inline properties. Adding a description or a new property to a bare `$ref` item requires restructuring to `allOf: [{$ref:...}, {properties:{remediation:{...}}}]` — but `common.schema.json` is explicitly out of scope and the plan does not describe this structural change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether the 'case-conditional' rubric in AC5 is implemented as (a) natural-language conditional instructions within the existing static `rubric:` string (no harness change) or (b) a structural YAML change — and if (b), confirm it does not require new eval harness behavior outside the stated scope.
findings.finding: AC5 requires the `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` llm-judge rubric to be made 'case-conditional', but the existing eval YAML format uses a single static `rubric:` string per scorer. The plan does not describe the YAML/format mechanism by which a rubric becomes case-conditional (e.g., a dict keyed by case type, a Jinja template, a separate rubric per scorer variant). Without specifying the format, it is unclear whether `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` would accept the new structure.
findings.suggested_fix: If natural-language conditionality is sufficient, state that explicitly: 'The rubric string is updated to include both the operator-attested and codebase-verifiable grounding requirements, with conditional language instructing the judge to apply the appropriate bar based on the case type.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC5 HOW the llm-judge knows which rubric branch to apply for a given case — e.g., embed a case-type signal the judge can observe (case id prefix, a `kind:` field in the dataset case, or explicit conditional prose that references an observable property of the output/case), and confirm this mechanism is present in the YAML.
findings.finding: AC5's case-conditional llm-judge rubric lacks a specified mechanism for the judge to determine, at scoring time, which branch of the conditional to apply for a given (case, output) pair. The rubric is a single static YAML string, and the judge scores one case at a time — if the rubric text contains a conditional ('for operator-attested cases, ticket-text citation counts; for codebase-verifiable cases, only file/symbol citations count'), the judge must know from context which case type it is evaluating. The plan says 'the rubric names the carve-out' but does not specify how the case type is surfaced to the judge (e.g., the rubric references the case id prefix, the case's ticket_context content, or the output's finding structure). Without this, the conditional is underspecified and the rubric may score both branches the same way.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `kind:` field (e.g., `kind: operator-attested` or `kind: codebase-verifiable`) to each of the four new dataset cases, and write the rubric conditional as: 'If the case kind is operator-attested (kind=operator-attested), a ticket-text source citation counts as valid grounding. If the case kind is codebase-verifiable (kind=codebase-verifiable or unset), only a file/symbol citation counts.' This way the judge can read the case kind from observable case metadata rather than inferring it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC5: specify the mechanism by which the pointwise llm-judge grader receives case-type context (operator-attested vs codebase-verifiable) — either (a) change the scorer from `pointwise: true` to case-level scoring so the grader receives the full case including `ticket_context`, or (b) require the verifier to annotate each finding with its criterion kind so the grader can detect it from the finding itself, or (c) document how the rubric text alone enables the grader to infer case type from the finding's citation kind.
findings.finding: AC5's `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` llm-judge scorer is `pointwise: true`, meaning Inspect AI feeds individual findings to the grader without case-level metadata. The plan says to make the rubric 'case-conditional' — operator-attested cases admit `source`/ticket-text citations, codebase-verifiable cases require file/symbol grounding — but never specifies how the pointwise grader will identify which branch applies for any given finding it receives. The case's `ticket_context` (which carries the `[operator-attested]` tag) is not part of the pointwise grader input, so the static rubric text alone cannot encode the conditional.

AC7's proving command (`test -f docs/adr/0043-... && grep -q 'operator-attested' CLAUDE.md`) verifies only file existence and a single token in CLAUDE.md, but AC7 specifies five distinct required content elements for the ADR: (1) two-kinds model + where DONE evidence lives, (2) bounded meaning of signed verdict, (3) haste-not-deception threat model WITH an explicit note on indirect-prompt-injection prior art and why the command/attestation split is acceptable, (4) concrete-vs-vague discriminator with ≥3 canonical gray-zone examples, (5) why external tools/collector are out of scope. A stub ADR with one occurrence of 'operator-attested' passes the proof command, so the five content requirements are unverifiable at close time.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the AC7 proof block with a compound command that anchors each required section:
```
test -f docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md \
  && grep -qi 'operator-attested' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md \
  && grep -qi 'prompt.inject' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md \
  && grep -qi 'gray.zone\|grey.zone\|example' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md \
  && grep -qi 'external tool\|out of scope' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md \
  && grep -qi 'bounded\|honest guarantee\|ticket.*codebase' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md \
  && grep -q 'operator-attested' CLAUDE.md
```
Alternatively, add a note: 'structural proof only — content completeness verified by PR reviewer against the five AC7 items.'

findings.finding: AC7's text demands rich ADR content — indirect-prompt-injection prior art, ≥3 canonical gray-zone examples, why external tools/collector are out of scope, the bounded-verdict meaning, and the haste-not-deception threat model — but the Proof only checks file existence (`test -f docs/adr/0043-…`) and a single CLAUDE.md grep. The substantial content requirements in the AC body go entirely unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC7 Proof with: `test -f docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md && grep -qi 'injection' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md && grep -qi 'gray' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md && grep -qi 'external' docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md && grep -q 'operator-attested' CLAUDE.md`

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
